rewrites.newText: Given a painful shock by electric current
rewrites.targetsMisreading: literal electricity reading of the root word
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage uses 'electrified' figuratively for the scientists' thrill at the discovery, not a physical electric shock

rewrites.newText: Made furious and resentful by the discovery
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the scientists were delighted and the find made headlines; nothing suggests anger

rewrites.newText: Left puzzled and unable to understand the find
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'electrified' signals eager excitement, not confusion; the meaning was clear enough to name and publicise it

rewrites.newText: Not interested in continuing her fossil work
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'undeterred' means she pressed on with fierce determination, the opposite of losing interest

rewrites.newText: Not properly trained or taught in science
rewrites.targetsMisreading: association trap with the passage's mention of her lack of education
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'undeterred' describes her refusal to be put off, not her level of schooling

rewrites.newText: Not believed or trusted by the scientific community
rewrites.targetsMisreading: confusion with the later Plesiosaurus doubters
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'undeterred' means not discouraged; being disbelieved is a different idea from a different paragraph

rewrites.newText: The family was carefully setting aside money to protect their future security
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'teetered on the edge of financial ruin' means they were almost broke, not building up savings

rewrites.newText: The family literally lived in a cottage perched on the crumbling cliff edge
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the phrase is figurative and about money; it does not describe where they physically lived

rewrites.newText: The family had recently lost a small amount of money in a bad deal
rewrites.targetsMisreading: near-miss with the wrong severity and tense
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'teetered' describes constant closeness to complete ruin, not a single small past loss

rewrites.newText: The family stood on the brink of suddenly becoming extremely rich and comfortable for life
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the phrase means about to lose everything, not about to gain great wealth

rewrites.newText: Not needed or important to her
rewrites.targetsMisreading: wrong shade (unnecessary rather than hard to get)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'elusive' means difficult to obtain; recognition was wanted, so 'not needed' is wrong

rewrites.newText: Something she actively did not want to receive
rewrites.targetsMisreading: wrong shade (unwanted rather than hard to get)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: she sought recognition; 'elusive' means it kept slipping out of reach, not that it was undesired

rewrites.newText: Quickly lost from people's memory over the years
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'elusive' means hard to catch or achieve, not something that faded from memory

rewrites.newText: A department run by the national government
rewrites.targetsMisreading: association trap with the governments that signed the treaty
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the project was privately funded with no taxpayer money; a consortium is a group of companies and banks, not a government body

rewrites.newText: A large fine charged for breaking the rules
rewrites.targetsMisreading: false connection to the project's overspending
rewrites.wrongnessReason: a consortium is a collaborating group of businesses, not a financial penalty

rewrites.newText: Leaving the body completely worn out
rewrites.targetsMisreading: swapping mental strain for physical strain
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'nerve-wracking' means causing anxiety or tension, not physical exhaustion

rewrites.newText: The ability to see clearly in the complete darkness of the deep
rewrites.targetsMisreading: association trap with the dark deep-sea setting
rewrites.wrongnessReason: bioluminescence is producing light from the body, not enhanced night vision

rewrites.newText: The ability to change the body's shape to escape from hungry predators
rewrites.targetsMisreading: confusion with a different deep-sea adaptation
rewrites.wrongnessReason: bioluminescence is about generating light, not altering body shape

rewrites.newText: The ability to light up only at the moment of being touched
rewrites.targetsMisreading: near-miss keeping 'light' but adding a wrong trigger
rewrites.wrongnessReason: deep-sea animals generate light from within to attract prey and communicate, not only reactively when touched

rewrites.newText: The ability to sense the body heat given off by other nearby creatures
rewrites.wrongnessReason: bioluminescence produces light, it is not a sense for detecting heat

rewrites.newText: Filled with fear and a desire to flee
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'captivated' means charmed and fascinated, a positive holding of attention, not frightened

rewrites.newText: Physically caught and held prisoner against its will
rewrites.wrongnessReason: here 'captivated' is figurative (it held human imagination), not a physical capture

rewrites.newText: Left confused and unable to make any sense of it
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'captivated' means fascinated and absorbed, not puzzled

rewrites.newText: It was a thrilling and completely unexpected surprise for everyone
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'sobering' means it made people think seriously about a worrying issue, not that it was thrilling

rewrites.newText: It helped scientists better understand the crushing pressure of the deep
rewrites.targetsMisreading: association trap with the passage's talk of pressure
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'sobering' describes the serious realisation about pollution reaching everywhere, not a lesson about pressure

rewrites.newText: Highly useful to researchers studying the disaster
rewrites.targetsMisreading: swapping emotional impact for practical usefulness
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'haunting' describes how deeply moving and unforgettable the casts are, not their scientific usefulness

rewrites.newText: Extremely old and dating from ancient times
rewrites.targetsMisreading: association trap with the ancient Roman setting
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'intricate' means elaborately detailed, not aged

rewrites.newText: Very large and covering a great area
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'intricate' refers to fine, complex detail, not physical size

rewrites.newText: Made from rare and very costly precious materials
rewrites.targetsMisreading: association with the wealthy merchants' houses
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'intricate' describes detailed design, not the expense of the materials

rewrites.newText: Concealed underneath the floor and completely out of sight
rewrites.targetsMisreading: literal reading of 'mosaic floors' as hidden
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'intricate' describes the visible detailed patterns of the floors, not something buried or hidden

rewrites.newText: A thrilling sense of eager and happy anticipation
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'dread' is fearful anxiety; Aisha is unhappy about the move, the opposite of eager anticipation

rewrites.newText: A strong and eager desire to explore something new
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'dread' means fear, not an eager wish to explore

rewrites.newText: A heavy feeling of being completely physically worn out
rewrites.targetsMisreading: swapping an emotion for physical tiredness
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'dread' is fear or anxiety about something coming, not bodily fatigue

rewrites.newText: Perfectly clear and easy to see straight through completely
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'translucent' lets only some light through and is not fully see-through, unlike clear glass

rewrites.newText: Brightly sparkling and shining with many different bright rainbow colours
rewrites.targetsMisreading: association with the shimmering water imagery
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'translucent' is about how much light passes through, not about sparkling or colour

rewrites.newText: A deep pool of water at the base
rewrites.targetsMisreading: association trap with the waterfall and pool nearby
rewrites.wrongnessReason: a 'fissure' is a narrow crack in rock, not a pool of water

rewrites.newText: Extremely convincing and hard to resist
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'unpersuasive' means NOT convincing, the exact opposite

rewrites.newText: Confusing and very difficult to understand
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'unpersuasive' means failing to convince, not hard to understand

rewrites.newText: Extremely dangerous and full of serious risk
rewrites.targetsMisreading: association with the risky cave situation
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'unpersuasive' refers to lacking convincing force, not to danger

rewrites.newText: Completely impossible to actually carry out in practice
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'unpersuasive' means it did not persuade him, not that the action could not be done

rewrites.newText: A dark shadow cast onto the ground by sunlight
rewrites.wrongnessReason: a 'silhouette' is the dark outline of the object itself against a bright background, not a shadow thrown on the ground

rewrites.newText: A photograph taken of the old tower at very close range
rewrites.wrongnessReason: a 'silhouette' is a dark outline shape, not a photograph

rewrites.newText: A particular style of tall stone building found along the coast
rewrites.targetsMisreading: confusing the outline with the lighthouse itself
rewrites.wrongnessReason: a 'silhouette' is the dark shape of the lighthouse seen against the sky, not a type of building

rewrites.newText: Rising to an extremely great and impressive height above the surrounding rocks
rewrites.targetsMisreading: association with a lighthouse being tall
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'decrepit' means worn out through age and neglect, not tall

rewrites.newText: Freshly painted and looking bright, clean and in completely new condition again
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite of run-down (the passage notes peeling paint)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'decrepit' means in poor, neglected condition, the opposite of freshly restored

rewrites.newText: Deeply frightened and constantly afraid of an approaching attack
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'complacent' means smugly untroubled and blind to danger; the townspeople felt untouchable, the opposite of frightened

rewrites.newText: Calm and quiet acceptance of being ruled
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'defiance' is bold resistance and refusal to obey, the opposite of quiet acceptance

rewrites.newText: Great physical strength and raw power used in battle
rewrites.targetsMisreading: association with a warrior queen's fighting
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'defiance' is an attitude of resistance, not physical strength

rewrites.newText: Complete agreement with the wishes of those in authority
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'defiance' means refusing to obey, the opposite of agreement with authority

rewrites.newText: A deep and lasting fear of those who hold great power
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'defiance' is bold resistance to power, not fear of it

rewrites.newText: Genuinely brave and physically strong in danger
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'reckless' is a negative word for careless disregard of consequences, not the same as bravery

rewrites.newText: Lazy and showing no interest in anything
rewrites.targetsMisreading: association with him skipping church and shirking duty
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'reckless' means acting rashly without care for consequences, not being idle

rewrites.newText: Clever and cunning but not at all honest
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'reckless' means careless of consequences, not deceitful

rewrites.newText: Only found or discovered a short time ago
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'enduring' means lasting a very long time; the tale is centuries old, not newly discovered

rewrites.newText: Painful and difficult to get through or bear
rewrites.wrongnessReason: here 'enduring' means long-lasting, not the sense of hardship one must put up with

rewrites.newText: Great excitement and eager enthusiasm
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'controversy' is heated public disagreement or dispute, not positive excitement
